questions.question: What is the minimum duration of fever to suspect Kawasaki disease without other criteria?
questions.explanation: Fever of at least 5 days is an essential criterion for the diagnosis of Kawasaki disease, although it may be considered earlier if there are four or more clinical criteria.

questions.question: How many additional clinical criteria are required for the typical diagnosis with fever ≥5 days?
questions.explanation: The classic diagnosis of Kawasaki disease requires persistent fever for at least 5 days along with at least 4 of the 5 major clinical criteria.

questions.question: Which of the following is a major clinical criterion of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Polymorphic rash is one of the five main clinical criteria; the others include bilateral conjunctivitis, lip/oral cavity changes, extremity changes, and cervical lymphadenopathy.

questions.question: Which ocular finding is characteristic of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis is a main clinical criterion, typically painless and without secretion, differentiating it from infectious causes.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in the acute phase of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Initial standard treatment consists of intravenous immunoglobulin (2 g/kg) plus aspirin at anti-inflammatory doses to reduce the risk of coronary aneurysms.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication of untreated Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: The formation of aneurysms in the coronary arteries is the most serious complication, which can lead to myocardial infarction or sudden death, especially if timely treatment is not administered.

questions.question: Which of the following is a major clinical criterion in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis is a major clinical criterion in Kawasaki disease, unlike other infectious conjunctivitis that is usually exudative or purulent.

questions.question: What characteristic does conjunctivitis have in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Conjunctivitis in Kawasaki disease is bilateral, non-exudative (without secretion), painless and usually respects the limbus, differentiating it from infectious conjunctivitis.

questions.question: What is the typical duration of conjunctivitis in untreated Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Conjunctivitis in Kawasaki disease usually persists for 1 to 2 weeks if untreated, coinciding with the acute phase of the disease, and resolves with anti-inflammatory treatment.

questions.question: What other ocular finding may accompany conjunctivitis in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Mild anterior uveitis may accompany conjunctivitis in Kawasaki disease, although it is less common; The other findings are not characteristic of this pediatric vasculitis.

questions.question: What first-line treatment reduces the duration of conjunctivitis in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin is the first-line treatment in Kawasaki disease and reduces the duration of conjunctivitis along with other acute symptoms, in addition to preventing coronary aneurysms.

questions.question: What is the main differential diagnosis of conjunctivitis in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Viral conjunctivitis is the main differential diagnosis, but in Kawasaki it is bilateral, non-exudative and is associated with fever and other clinical criteria, unlike viral conjunctivitis which is usually exudative.

questions.question: Which of the following characteristics describes the polymorphic rash in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: The polymorphic rash in Kawasaki disease usually presents as diffuse erythematous macules, without vesicles or crusts, and may mimic other viral eruptions.

questions.question: What body distribution is most common in polymorphous Kawasaki rash?
questions.explanation: The polymorphous exanthema is typically generalized, predominantly on the trunk and extremities, and can be morbilliform or scarlatiniform, but it is not exclusive to the palms or unilateral.

questions.question: What is the main cause of the polymorphic rash in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: The polymorphous rash in Kawasaki is a consequence of a systemic vasculitis of small and medium vessels, not a direct infection or an allergy.

questions.question: What other major clinical criterion usually accompanies polymorphic rash in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Unilateral cervical adenopathy (>1.5 cm) is one of the classic criteria for Kawasaki disease, along with fever, conjunctivitis, mucosal changes, and rash.

questions.question: When does the polymorphic rash usually appear in the course of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: The polymorphous rash typically appears within the first 5 days of fever, as part of the acute phase of the disease, and is one of the early diagnostic criteria.

questions.question: What specific treatment is indicated for polymorphous rash in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin (2 g/kg) is the treatment of choice in Kawasaki disease to reduce the risk of coronary aneurysms, regardless of the rash.

questions.question: What is a typical feature of extremity changes in the acute phase of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: In the acute phase of Kawasaki disease, palmar and plantar erythema is a classic finding in the extremities, along with edema. Periungual peeling appears in the subacute phase.

questions.question: What extremity change is characteristic of the subacute phase of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Periungual peeling, which usually begins in the periungual region of the fingers, is a typical finding of the subacute phase (second to third week) of Kawasaki disease.

questions.question: Which of the following extremity findings supports the diagnosis of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Palmar and plantar erythema, together with edema, is one of the main clinical criteria in the acute phase of Kawasaki disease, differentiating it from other vasculitis.

questions.question: What change in extremities is most frequently observed in Kawasaki disease during the third week of evolution?
questions.explanation: Periungual peeling is characteristic of the subacute phase (around the third week) in Kawasaki disease, and usually begins on the fingers and then the toes.

questions.question: Edema of the hands and feet in Kawasaki disease is typically associated with which extremity finding?
questions.explanation: Edema of the hands and feet in the acute phase of Kawasaki disease is usually accompanied by palmar and plantar erythema, forming part of the clinical criteria of the disease.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical limb change in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Osler nodules are characteristic of infective endocarditis, not Kawasaki disease. Typical extremity changes include erythema, edema, and periungual peeling.

questions.question: Which of the following oral findings is characteristic of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Raspberry tongue (hypertrophic and erythematous papillae) is a classic sign of the acute phase of Kawasaki disease, along with erythema and lip fissures.

questions.question: What lip alteration is common in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Dry, erythematous, and chapped (fissured) lips are a minor clinical criterion in Kawasaki disease, reflecting mucocutaneous inflammation.

questions.question: What oral sign is associated with the subacute phase of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: In the subacute phase, peeling of the tongue can be observed, although the best-known sign is the raspberry tongue in the acute phase; Peeling is a less common but described finding.

questions.question: Which of the following is not an oral clinical criterion in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Ulceronecrotizing gingivitis is not part of the oral Kawasaki criteria; Typical findings are lip erythema, raspberry tongue, and pharyngeal erythema without ulcers.

questions.question: Which finding in the oral mucosa is typical in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Diffuse erythema of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa, without exudate or ulcers, is a characteristic finding of Kawasaki disease, reflecting systemic vasculitis.

questions.question: What percentage of patients with Kawasaki disease present oral disorders?
questions.explanation: Oral alterations (cracked lips, raspberry tongue, pharyngeal erythema) occur in approximately 80-90% of patients with Kawasaki disease, being one of the most sensitive diagnostic criteria.

questions.question: What is the characteristic of cervical adenopathy in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: In Kawasaki disease, cervical adenopathy is typically unilateral, painless and larger than 1.5 cm, being one of the main clinical criteria.

questions.question: What minimum size should cervical lymphadenopathy be to meet Kawasaki criteria?
questions.explanation: The clinical criterion for cervical adenopathy in Kawasaki requires a diameter greater than 1.5 cm, generally unilateral and painless.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a major Kawasaki clinical criterion?
questions.explanation: Cervical adenopathy in Kawasaki is unilateral, not bilateral. Bilaterality does not meet the criterion, which requires unilaterality and size >1.5 cm.

questions.question: How often does cervical adenopathy occur in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Cervical adenopathy is the least common criterion in Kawasaki, occurring in approximately 25-50% of cases, unlike other more common signs.

questions.question: What other pediatric pathology is the main differential diagnosis of cervical adenopathy in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Cervical bacterial adenitis is usually unilateral and painful, while in Kawasaki it is painless. Fever and other criteria help differentiate them.

questions.question: How many days of fever are required to consider cervical adenopathy as Kawasaki criteria?
questions.explanation: Fever must persist for at least 5 days to consider the diagnosis of Kawasaki disease, and cervical adenopathy is one of the additional criteria.

questions.question: What is the main cause of death in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Coronary aneurysms are the most serious complication of Kawasaki disease, and can cause thrombosis, myocardial infarction or sudden death.

questions.question: What study is the gold standard to detect coronary aneurysms in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography is the non-invasive method of choice to evaluate the coronary arteries during the acute phase and follow-up of Kawasaki disease.

questions.question: What diameter of coronary artery is considered giant aneurysm in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: A giant aneurysm is defined when the internal diameter of the coronary artery is greater than 8 mm, being associated with the highest risk of thrombotic complications.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment to prevent coronary aneurysms in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin (2 g/kg as a single dose) administered within the first 10 days significantly reduces the risk of developing coronary aneurysms.

questions.question: In what phase of Kawasaki disease are coronary aneurysms usually detected?
questions.explanation: Coronary aneurysms usually appear in the subacute phase (weeks 2 to 4), when fever subsides but the risk of progressive vascular injury persists.

questions.question: What factor is associated with increased risk of coronary aneurysms in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Persistent fever for more than 10 days without adequate treatment is the main risk factor for the development of coronary aneurysms in Kawasaki disease.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of intravenous immunoglobulin in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin acts mainly by modulating the inflammatory and immunological response, reducing vascular damage and the incidence of coronary aneurysms in Kawasaki disease.

questions.question: What dose of aspirin is recommended in the acute phase of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: In the acute phase, aspirin is used in high anti-inflammatory doses, 80-100 mg/kg/day, divided into four doses, to reduce inflammation and thrombotic risk.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication if IV immunoglobulin is not administered in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Without treatment with IV immunoglobulin, up to 25% of children develop coronary artery aneurysms, which is the most serious and characteristic complication of Kawasaki disease.

questions.question: When should intravenous immunoglobulin be administered in Kawasaki disease?
options: Only if there is fever >7 days
questions.explanation: IV immunoglobulin should ideally be administered within the first 10 days of fever, and as soon as possible, to minimize the risk of coronary aneurysms.

questions.question: What dose of aspirin is recommended in the convalescent phase of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: In the convalescent phase, the dose of aspirin is reduced to 3-5 mg/kg/day in a single dose, as an antiplatelet agent, until the risk of thrombosis decreases.

questions.question: What to do if fever persists 36 hours after the first dose of IV immunoglobulin?
questions.explanation: If fever persists or recurs more than 36 hours after the first dose, a second dose of IV immunoglobulin (2 g/kg) is recommended to control inflammation and reduce coronary risk.

questions.question: What is the dose of intravenous immunoglobulin in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: The first-line treatment in Kawasaki disease is intravenous immunoglobulin at 2 g/kg in a single dose, since it reduces the incidence of coronary aneurysms.

questions.question: What complication is prevented with immunoglobulin in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Early administration of intravenous immunoglobulin significantly reduces the risk of developing coronary aneurysms, the main complication of Kawasaki disease.

questions.question: In what range of days from the onset of fever should immunoglobulin be administered?
questions.explanation: Treatment with intravenous immunoglobulin is most effective when administered between days 5 and 10 of illness, ideally within the first 10 days of fever.

questions.question: What dose of aspirin is used in the acute phase of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: In the acute phase of Kawasaki disease, aspirin is used in anti-inflammatory doses of 30-50 mg/kg/day, divided into 3-4 doses, associated with immunoglobulin.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of immunoglobulin in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin exerts anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects, reducing endothelial activation and the production of proinflammatory cytokines in Kawasaki disease.

questions.question: What clinical criterion is NOT part of the diagnosis of classic Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Cervical adenomegaly in Kawasaki is typically unilateral and greater than 1.5 cm, not unique. Classic criteria include conjunctivitis, rash, extremity changes, and unilateral cervical adenomegaly.

questions.question: What is the anti-inflammatory dose of aspirin in Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: The anti-inflammatory dose of aspirin in Kawasaki disease is 30-50 mg/kg/day, recommended to reduce inflammation and prevent coronary complications in the acute phase.

questions.question: What is the purpose of aspirin in anti-inflammatory doses in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: In Kawasaki disease, aspirin at anti-inflammatory doses (30-50 mg/kg/day) is used to reduce systemic inflammation and the risk of coronary aneurysms during the acute phase.

questions.question: What is the typical duration of anti-inflammatory aspirin in Kawasaki?
options: Up to one year of life
questions.explanation: Aspirin at an anti-inflammatory dose is continued until the patient is afebrile for 48-72 hours and inflammatory markers improve, usually a few days, not prolonged weeks.

questions.question: What complication does anti-inflammatory aspirin prevent in Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Aspirin in anti-inflammatory doses reduces vascular inflammation and the formation of coronary aneurysms, the main complication of Kawasaki disease in children.

questions.question: What other drug is associated with aspirin in acute Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Standard treatment for the acute phase of Kawasaki combines anti-inflammatory aspirin with intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) to reduce inflammation and prevent coronary damage.

questions.question: In what age range is Kawasaki disease most common?
questions.explanation: Kawasaki disease mainly affects children between 6 months and 5 years, with a peak at 2 years, being rare in infants under 3 months and in adolescents.

questions.question: What is the main indication to suspect incomplete Kawasaki in a child with fever?
questions.explanation: Persistent fever for at least 5 days is the main criterion for suspecting incomplete Kawasaki disease, even without meeting complete clinical criteria.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding is characteristic of incomplete Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: In incomplete Kawasaki, reactive thrombocytosis is usually observed in the subacute phase, along with elevation of acute phase reactants such as CRP and ESR.

questions.question: What is the first line treatment in incomplete Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) is the first-line treatment in incomplete Kawasaki, as it significantly reduces the risk of coronary aneurysms.

questions.question: Which clinical criterion is most frequently absent in incomplete Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Cervical adenopathy (greater than 1.5 cm) is the least common criterion in incomplete Kawasaki, which makes the diagnosis difficult if all the classic signs are expected.

questions.question: What study is recommended to perform if incomplete Kawasaki is suspected with persistent fever?
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography is essential to evaluate the presence of coronary abnormalities, such as dilation or aneurysms, in patients with suspected incomplete Kawasaki.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication of untreated incomplete Kawasaki?
questions.explanation: Coronary aneurysm is the most serious complication of untreated incomplete Kawasaki, as it can lead to myocardial infarction or sudden death in childhood.

questions.question: When should the first echocardiogram be performed in a patient with suspected Kawasaki disease?
options: On the seventh day of fever
questions.explanation: The initial echocardiogram should be performed within the first week of fever onset to evaluate for the presence of coronary aneurysms and other early cardiac abnormalities.

questions.question: What is the main echocardiographic alteration in the acute phase of Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: In the acute phase, dilation of the coronary arteries is the most characteristic alteration and can progress to aneurysms if not treated promptly.

questions.question: Which echocardiographic finding is an indication to repeat the study after 2 weeks?
questions.explanation: The presence of a coronary aneurysm in the initial echocardiogram requires echocardiographic follow-up after 2 weeks to evaluate its evolution and possible regression.

questions.question: What is the minimum echocardiographic follow-up time in patients without coronary alterations?
questions.explanation: In patients without coronary alterations at diagnosis, a control echocardiogram is recommended after one year to rule out the late development of aneurysms.

questions.question: What echocardiographic parameter defines a coronary aneurysm in children?
questions.explanation: In pediatrics, a coronary artery diameter greater than 3 mm is considered aneurysmal, although it is adjusted according to the patient's body size.

questions.question: What complementary study is recommended if the echocardiogram is doubtful for evaluating coronary arteries?
questions.explanation: Coronary angiography is the gold standard for evaluating coronary anatomy when echocardiogram is inconclusive or complex aneurysms are suspected.
